title: Sethi Mohallah

infobox.value: ([Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"): سیٹھی محلہ), sometimes called **Sethian Mohallah** and **Kucha Sethian**, is an old [mohallah](/wiki/Mahallah "Mahallah") (traditional neighbourhood) in the walled city of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). The neighbourhood contains seven surviving [havelis](/wiki/Haveli "Haveli") (mansions) built by the [Sethi](/wiki/Sethi "Sethi") family in a style reminiscent of Central Asia with elaborate wooden carvings. The houses were built over a period of one century, from c. 1800 – 1910.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Neighbourhood in Peshawar, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan

| Sethi Mohallah   سیٹھی محلہ | |
| --- | --- |
| [Neighbourhood](/wiki/Neighbourhood "Neighbourhood") | |
| [The Sethi Mohallah is famous for its Central-Asian style homes.](/wiki/File:The_Enchanting_Sethi_House.jpg "The Sethi Mohallah is famous for its Central-Asian style homes.") The Sethi Mohallah is famous for its Central-Asian style homes. | |
| Map Interactive map of Sethi Mohallah   سیٹھی محلہ | |
| Country | Pakistan |
| Province | [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") |
| City | [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") |

**Sethi Mohallah** ([Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"): سیٹھی محلہ), sometimes called **Sethian Mohallah** and **Kucha Sethian**, is an old [mohallah](/wiki/Mahallah "Mahallah") (traditional neighbourhood) in the walled city of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). The neighbourhood contains seven surviving [havelis](/wiki/Haveli "Haveli") (mansions) built by the [Sethi](/wiki/Sethi "Sethi") family in a style reminiscent of Central Asia with elaborate wooden carvings. The houses were built over a period of one century, from c. 1800 – 1910.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sethi_Mohallah&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Sethi_House_-_second_floor.jpg/250px-Sethi_House_-_second_floor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sethi_House_-_second_floor.jpg)

A view of one of the Sethi Mohallah's courtyards

The Sethis were a prominent [Punjabi Muslim](/wiki/Punjabi_Muslim "Punjabi Muslim") merchant family with origins in the city of [Bhera](/wiki/Bhera "Bhera"), in [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2016131-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) From Bhera Sethis primarily exported [indigo](/wiki/Indigo_dye "Indigo dye") to central Asia. In 1660, Mian Hafiz Ghulam Ahmad, the patriarch of the family, moved to [Chamkani](/wiki/Chamkani,_Peshawar "Chamkani, Peshawar"), a town near Peshawar, to expand the family trade. His son Hafiz Fazal Ahmad moved to Peshawar in 1730. In the following decades the Sethis were firmly established in the city. Over time Sethis became very prosperous, trading not only in indigo but also in wood, fur, [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") cloth, [copperware](/wiki/Copperware "Copperware"), [salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt"), [spices](/wiki/Spices "Spices"), [porcelain](/wiki/Porcelain "Porcelain"), [carpets](/wiki/Carpet "Carpet") and gold thread.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2016131–132-3) They relied on local as well as international trade – their connections went as far as [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") and Central Asia. The Sethi family was involved in considerable welfare work in Peshawar and had contributed to the construction of wells for the poor, along with the [Lady Reading Hospital](/wiki/Lady_Reading_Hospital "Lady Reading Hospital") and the [Islamia College](/wiki/Islamia_College_(Peshawar) "Islamia College (Peshawar)") Mosque.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

The Sethi family shifted to Mohalla Sethian in the early 19th century, and in the following decades built a series of havelis. The downfall of the Sethis began during the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") in 1917, when their businesses experienced setbacks from which they never recovered, forcing them to leave Central Asia and return to Peshawar.

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sethi_Mohallah&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Location")]

It is situated close to the [Ghanta Ghar](/wiki/Cunningham_clock_tower "Cunningham clock tower") (clock tower), Bazaar Kalan and [Gor Khuttree](/wiki/Gor_Khuttree "Gor Khuttree") in Peshawar's old walled city.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sethi_Mohallah&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Design")]

The homes were designed with inspiration from the vernacular architecture of [Bukhara](/wiki/Bukhara "Bukhara"). Two neatly decorated tehkhanas (basement rooms), a balakhana (upper storey), dalaans (big halls), chinikhanas (rooms where decoration and art pieces are displayed on chimneypieces) and fountains can be found in each house. The ceilings are painted and the walls are decorated with mirror work. One of the houses has been purchased by the NWFP government, this house has two portions, one for men and one for women. The overall structure is a combination of brick and woodwork embellished with carved wooden doors and balconies. Painted and mirrored atriums provide fresh air and attractive views, while the main entrances are made of superbly carved wood.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

It has 12 rooms and four basements and includes a fountain. Colourful glass ceilings with geometrical designs, woodwork with engravings of various splendid designs on walls, a big resting place, made of wood and called Takht-e-Sulaimani, to take sunshine in winter, beautiful ventilators, slanting window-shades, wooden wall cupboards, chimneys and red bricks stairs with projected wooden frames are some of the prominent features of the house.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

## List of Havelis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sethi_Mohallah&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: List of Havelis")]

The Sethi family built twelve *havelis* from the early 19th century to 1910, out of which seven survive today with some modifications:[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2016134–147-7)

* Muhammad Akram house (early 1800s), now demolished
* Karam Buksh Sethi *haveli* (1808–1814), earliest surviving mansion
* Fateh Gul Sethi *haveli* (1810–1818)
* Ahmad Gul *haveli* (1823*–*1830s)
* Elahi Buksh Sethi *haveli* (1832*–*1840s), demolished in 1970s
* Haji Saeed Ahmad Sethi *haveli* (1850s), demolished in 1980s
* Karim Buksh Sethi *haveli* (1889*–*1901)
* Abdul Rahim Sethi *haveli* (1890*–*1896)
* Fateh Gul Sethi's grandson *haveli* (1890s), demolished in 1980s
* Abdur Karim Sethi *haveli* (1901*–*1905)
* *Haveli* of the Fateh Gul family (1900s), demolished in 1950s
* Abdul Jalil Sethi *haveli* (1904*–*1910)
